name: Bellevue
short_description: BLM public land in the Wood River Valley offering easy rockhounding for agate, chalcedony, and quartz. No permit required, 25 lb daily limit on gentle terrain at 5948 ft elevation.
description:
Bellevue sits on productive BLM land in Blaine County's Wood River Valley, a geologically rich region known for silica-rich mineral deposits. The site is characterized by gentle slopes and accessible terrain at nearly 6000 feet elevation, making it suitable for rockhounders of varying experience levels.

The Bellevue area sits within the Basin and Range province where Tertiary volcanic and hydrothermal activity created abundant silica deposits. Hot spring and geothermal fluids moving through fractured rocks deposited layers of chalcedony, agate, and microcrystalline quartz during the Miocene epoch. These processes created the nodules and seams you'll find scattered throughout washes and exposed hillsides.

Expect to find small to medium agate nodules in washes and along exposed geologic contacts. Chalcedony fragments and banded specimens are common in gravelly areas. The gentle slope terrain allows you to cover reasonable ground without strenuous hiking. Most productive collecting occurs in drainage washes where erosion naturally exposes fresh material.

The BLM designation means free public access with minimal regulation. Bring tools for surface collecting - a crack hammer and chisels work well for extracting specimens from outcrop. This is an excellent location for families and beginners due to the accessible terrain and abundance of colorful specimens.

Respect the 25 lb daily limit and leave the area clean. Pack out all waste and fill in any small holes you dig. The combination of easy access, no fees, and reliable collecting makes Bellevue a favorite for Wood River Valley rockhounders.
history: This area sits within the Basin and Range province where Tertiary-age volcanic activity and associated hydrothermal circulation deposited abundant silica-rich fluids that formed chalcedony, agate, and quartz nodules. Local prospecting activity in the 19th and early 20th centuries focused on precious metals, but the widespread silica deposits made the region attractive for recreational rockhounding by the mid-1900s.
tips: Search drainage washes first - erosion naturally exposes fresh specimens and concentrates heavier agates in gravel bars. Look for the characteristic translucent to milky white outer layer of agate nodules among darker host rock. Use a 3-4 lb crack hammer and cold chisel to carefully extract specimens from shallow outcrops; strike perpendicular to natural fractures rather than directly on the agate. Bring a 5-gallon bucket with a screen to sort material from productive wash areas and identify pieces worth extracting.
terrain_notes: Gentle sloping terrain at 5948 ft elevation with sagebrush and scattered ponderosa pine. Walking distance from parking is typically under 0.5 miles to productive collecting areas. Footing is moderately rocky but stable; loose pumice and volcanic debris interspersed with clay soils. Washes provide the easiest walking; hillsides require moderate scrambling over loose rock.
written_directions: From Ketchum, travel south on ID 75 toward Sun Valley for approximately 12 miles. Continue south past Sun Valley toward Bellevue; the community of Bellevue is located along ID 75 in the Wood River Valley. Access to BLM collecting areas is available from pullouts and informal parking areas along and near ID 75 in the Bellevue vicinity. Look for wash drainages and exposed hillsides accessible from road pullouts. Exact BLM access points vary; consult the Shoshone BLM Field Office or local rockhounding groups for current public access parking locations and productive zones.
road_conditions: ID 75 is a well-maintained paved highway. Access roads to specific collecting areas may be unpaved/dirt with good clearance for standard vehicles during dry season.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along ID 75 and adjacent dirt roads. Limited formal parking facilities. Capacity varies by location; typically 2-6 vehicles per pullout. Plan to arrive early during peak season (weekends in summer).
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or mechanized equipment. All collecting must be hand tool based. Leave the site clean and undisturbed. Respect private property boundaries.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation or heavy equipment
Hand tools only
Pack out all trash
Fill in holes after digging
nearest_services: Bellevue and Sun Valley (approximately 10-15 miles north) have gas, food, lodging, and supplies. Ketchum (approximately 20 miles north) has full services including rockhound-friendly shops.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently sloping terrain accessible to most hikers and families. Informal pullout parking with short walks (typically under 0.5 miles) to collecting areas. Terrain is rocky with loose pumice but stable underfoot. No wheelchair accessibility due to unpaved parking areas and rocky footing, but the gentle slope accommodates most fitness levels. Sturdy footwear and water recommended.

faq.question: What types of agates and chalcedony can I find rockhounding near Bellevue, Idaho?
faq.answer: Bellevue's BLM land produces small to medium banded agates, translucent to milky chalcedony specimens, and crystalline quartz. Most pieces are 0.5 to 3 inches in size. You may also find jasper and colored quartz fragments. Surface collecting in washes yields the most accessible material; expect to find 5-15 specimens per outing.

faq.question: Is a permit required for rockhounding on BLM land at Bellevue?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding on BLM land at Bellevue. It is free, public access land managed under FLPMA regulations. You only need to follow the 25 lb daily collection limit and use hand tools. No special registration or fees apply.

faq.question: What is the best time of year for rockhounding in the Bellevue area?
faq.answer: June through September is the optimal season. Higher elevation (5948 ft) means snow typically blocks access from October through May. Summer provides mild temperatures, dry conditions, and reliable access to BLM collecting areas. Avoid July peak season on weekends if you prefer solitude.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding at Bellevue?
faq.answer: Hand tools are required - bring a 3-4 lb crack hammer, cold chisels, and a small pry bar for extracting specimens from outcrop. A 5-gallon bucket with screening is useful for sorting wash material. Wear sturdy boots for rocky terrain and bring plenty of water. A backpack, rock bucket, and gloves are helpful for extended collecting sessions.

faq.question: Is Bellevue a good rockhounding location for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes, Bellevue is excellent for beginners and families. The terrain is gentle with short walks to productive areas. Surface collecting yields specimens without requiring special skills. The 25 lb daily limit prevents over-collecting and the free BLM access means no fees. Kids ages 6+ enjoy the easy hiking and satisfaction of finding colorful agates.

faq.question: How far is Bellevue rockhounding from Ketchum, and what are nearby services?
faq.answer: Bellevue is approximately 20 miles south of Ketchum via ID 75. Ketchum offers gas, food, lodging, and supplies. The nearby town of Bellevue (on ID 75) and Sun Valley have limited services. Plan to bring adequate water and supplies, as the collecting area itself has no facilities. Cell service is spotty, so download maps offline.

meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding near Bellevue, Idaho. Find agate, chalcedony, and quartz. No permit, 25 lb/day limit. Easy terrain at 5948 ft elevation. Perfect for families.
